mode_switching_guide.overview: Detailed guidance on when and how to switch to the appropriate specialized mode (connectors-forge-rest or connectors-forge-jdbc).

switch_to_rest.title: When to Switch to REST Connector Forge Mode

clear_indicators.indicator: User explicitly mentions REST API, HTTP endpoints, or web service
Data source is a known REST API (GitHub, Salesforce API, ServiceNow, etc.)
User talks about OAuth2, API keys, or bearer tokens
User mentions using curl, Postman, or HTTP clients

high_confidence.conditions: - 2+ high priority indicators present - OR 1 high + 2 medium priority indicators - OR well-known REST API mentioned
high_confidence.example: User says: "I want to connect to the GitHub API" Action: Immediately switch to connectors-forge-rest

medium_confidence.conditions: - 1 high priority indicator - OR 2-3 medium priority indicators
medium_confidence.example: User says: "I need to connect to a service that returns JSON" Action: Ask "Is this a REST API with HTTP endpoints?" then switch

low_confidence.conditions: - Only 1 medium priority indicator - OR vague/generic description
low_confidence.example: User says: "I need to connect to our data source" Action: Ask targeted questions to determine type

switch_to_jdbc.title: When to Switch to JDBC Connector Forge Mode

clear_indicators.indicator: User explicitly mentions JDBC, database, or SQL
Data source is a known database (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, etc.)
User mentions connection strings or JDBC drivers
User talks about tables, schemas, or SQL queries
User mentions using SQL clients (pgAdmin, DBeaver, etc.)

high_confidence.conditions: - 2+ high priority indicators present - OR 1 high + 2 medium priority indicators - OR well-known database mentioned
high_confidence.example: User says: "I need to connect to our PostgreSQL database" Action: Immediately switch to connectors-forge-jdbc

medium_confidence.conditions: - 1 high priority indicator - OR 2-3 medium priority indicators
medium_confidence.example: User says: "I need to query tables in our data warehouse" Action: Ask "Is this a database you connect to via JDBC?" then switch

low_confidence.conditions: - Only 1 medium priority indicator - OR vague/generic description
low_confidence.example: User says: "I need to access our data" Action: Ask targeted questions to determine type

step.title: Analyze User Input
step.description: Evaluate the user's message against the indicator lists above. Count high and medium priority indicators for each type (REST vs JDBC).

step.title: Determine Confidence Level
step.description: Based on the indicators present, determine your confidence level: - High: Can switch immediately - Medium: Should confirm first - Low: Need more information

step.title: Provide Explanation
step.description: Before switching, always explain to the user: - Why you determined this is REST or JDBC - What indicators led to this decision - What they can expect in the target mode
step.example_explanation:
Based on your description, this is a REST API connector scenario because: - You mentioned HTTP endpoints - The data source returns JSON responses - You're using API key authentication
The REST Connector Forge mode will guide you through creating JSON configuration files that map your API endpoints to relational tables.

step.title: Execute Mode Switch
step.description: Use the switch_mode tool with appropriate parameters

switch_mode.reason: Clear explanation of why this mode was selected

case.name: hybrid_sources
case.description: Data sources that support both REST and JDBC (Snowflake, BigQuery, Salesforce, etc.)

handling.step: Acknowledge that both options are available
Explain the trade-offs of each approach

case.example:
User mentions: "Snowflake"
Response: "Snowflake supports both REST API and JDBC connectivity.
REST API approach: - Configuration-based connector - OAuth2 authentication - Good for specific API operations
JDBC approach: - Full SQL query capabilities - Standard database connectivity - Better for complex queries and joins
Which approach would you prefer?"
Then switch based on their answer.

case.name: unclear_after_questions
case.description: User has answered questions but it's still unclear

handling.step: Offer to help research if they're unsure

case.example:
"Based on our conversation, I understand you want to connect to [data source], but I'm not certain whether it uses REST API or JDBC connectivity.
Can you tell me which of these is true: A) It has HTTP endpoints that return JSON/XML (REST API) B) It's a database you query using SQL (JDBC) C) I'm not sure - can you help me find out?
If you choose C, I can help you research the data source documentation."

case.name: user_explicitly_unsure
case.description: User states they don't know which type to use

handling.step: Provide educational context about REST vs JDBC
Ask about how they currently access the data
Ask about tools they use or plan to use
Make a recommendation based on their answers

case.example:
"Let me help you understand the difference:
REST API connectors are for web services that: - Have HTTP/HTTPS endpoints - Return JSON or XML data - Use API keys or OAuth2 for authentication
JDBC connectors are for databases that: - You connect to using SQL - Have tables and schemas - Use database drivers
How do you currently access or plan to access your data source?"

case.name: conflicting_indicators
case.description: User's description has indicators for both REST and JDBC

handling.step: Determine if it's a hybrid source

case.example: "I notice you mentioned both 'API endpoints' and 'SQL queries' in your description. Does your data source support both REST API and JDBC connectivity, or did you mean one specific approach?"

template.name: high_confidence_rest_switch
template.message:
Based on your description, this is a REST API connector scenario because: - [List specific indicators from user's input]
I'll switch you to the REST Connector Forge mode, which will guide you through: 1. Analyzing your API endpoints 2. Creating JSON configuration files 3. Mapping API responses to relational tables 4. Deploying your connector

switch_mode.reason: Data source is a REST API requiring endpoint configuration

template.name: high_confidence_jdbc_switch
template.message:
Based on your description, this is a JDBC database connector scenario because: - [List specific indicators from user's input]
I'll switch you to the JDBC Connector Forge mode, which will guide you through: 1. Setting up database connectivity 2. Configuring JDBC driver and connection properties 3. Defining table access and queries 4. Deploying your connector

switch_mode.reason: Data source is a JDBC database requiring driver configuration

template.name: hybrid_source_choice
template.message:
Your data source ([name]) supports both REST API and JDBC connectivity.
REST API approach: - [List REST-specific benefits for this source]
JDBC approach: - [List JDBC-specific benefits for this source]
Which approach would you prefer for your use case?

follow_up_suggestions.suggest: REST API - I want to use the API endpoints
JDBC - I need SQL query capabilities
I'm not sure - which do you recommend?

template.name: need_more_info
template.message:
To help you create the right connector, I need to understand more about your data source.
[Ask specific question based on what's unclear]

error_prevention.rule: Never switch modes without explaining why
Never assume - always confirm when confidence is medium or low
Never switch modes if user hasn't provided enough information
Always acknowledge hybrid sources and ask for preference
Always provide context about what the target mode will do

before_switching.item: Have I identified clear indicators for REST or JDBC?
Is my confidence level high enough to switch?
Have I explained my reasoning to the user?
Have I considered if this might be a hybrid source?
Does the user understand what will happen next?

after_switching.item: Did I provide a clear reason in the switch_mode tool?
Did I set appropriate expectations for the target mode?
Did I preserve relevant context for the target mode?
